response.title: Nuclear Fusion Energy Breakthrough: Ignition Confirmed in Record...
response.snippet: February 15, 2024. On August 8, 2021, for the first time in decades of inertial confinement fusion research, a yield of more than 1.3 megajoules (MJ) was realized.

response.title: French fusion reactor stuns world by sustaining nuclear reaction for...
response.snippet: Send through Whatsapp. The quest for fusion energy has taken a historic turn as scientists push the boundaries of what’s possible with sustained plasma. With each new record, the promise of clean, abundant power inches closer to reality.
response.content: French fusion reactor stuns world by sustaining nuclear reaction for 22 minutes in breakthrough energy milestone - Glass Almanac Skip to content © French fusion reactor stuns world by sustaining nuclear reaction for 22 minutes in breakthrough energy milestone Share this post Post on Facebook Post on Flipboard Share on LinkedIn Post on Reddit Share on Threads Share on X Send through Whatsapp The quest for fusion energy has taken a historic turn as scientists push the boundaries of what’s possible with sustained plasma. With each new record, the promise of clean, abundant power inches closer to reality. The Dream of Fusion Power Subscribe to the Glass Almanac YouTube channel Last spring, I stood beside a model of the sun at a science fair and couldn’t help but marvel: could we bottle that limitless energy? For over 80 years, researchers have chased fusion —the process that powers our star—seeking a net positive reaction that would transform our sustainable energy landscape. Imagine: one gram of deuterium-tritium fuel generating 90,000 kilowatt-hours of energy—equivalent to 11 tonnes of coal¹—without producing greenhouse gases. Yet, reproducing the sun’s conditions—temperatures above 100 million Celsius, immense pressures, and perfectly stable plasma —has remained elusive. Governments and private companies have poured billions into tokamaks , lasers, and novel confinement methods, all racing toward the same prize: clean, virtually inexhaustible power. Breaking Records at the WEST Tokamak On February 12, a team at France’s CEA announced they had kept a plasma reaction alive for an astonishing 1,337 seconds—over 22 minutes—inside the WEST Tokamak reactor². I remember reading the announcement over a late-night coffee: these numbers didn’t just surpass China’s previous 1,066-second record; they signaled a leap in reactor stability and component durability. response.title: The Elephant in The Fusion Room is…When? | by Shaun... | Medium
response.snippet: Will fusion energy deliver abundant and cost-effective electricity? How fast can we deploy fusion to fulfill our energy appetite? We are seeing an acceleration of operational breakthroughs, increased global funding, and competition from China, which is motivating the West to win this race.
